Project Name (pick one): AquaAlert / ClearWaters / HydroWatch / TideGuard / BlueWatch
Tagline: "Track the source, save the water."
Problem Statement:
Water bodies near us — beaches, lakes, and green tourist spots — are getting polluted, but the pollution rarely starts in the water. It starts on land: illegal dumping near water bodies and broken drains/manholes that discharge overflow directly into them. There's no easy way for citizens to report these hidden causes before they turn into visible, large-scale contamination.
Core Idea:
A citizen-reporting platform where anyone can report pollution at a water body, tag why it's happening (source-tagging), and see it plotted live on a map — turning invisible causes into trackable, prioritized data for local authorities.
Main Focus: Water body pollution (beaches, lakes, seas, tourist green spots)
Contributing-cause tags (secondary features, same report form):

🚱 Illegal dumping near/into water body
🕳️ Broken drain/manhole discharging into water body
🏖️ General visible pollution (plastic, sewage, debris)

Core User Flow:

User opens app/site → taps "Report Pollution"
Uploads photo + auto-captures geolocation
Selects cause tag (dumping / drain overflow / general pollution) + severity (low/medium/high)
Report appears instantly as a pin on a live map, color-coded by cause
Community can upvote to show urgency
Status field simulates authority response: Reported → Acknowledged → Cleaned

Key Screens to Build:

Report form (photo, location, cause tag, severity)
Live map view (pins clustered/colored by cause type)
Issue detail view (photo, status, upvotes)
Simple dashboard/stats (e.g., "12 reports this week, 60% drain-related")

Why It Matters (impact points to use in pitch):

Protects public health (waterborne disease risk)
Protects tourism & local economy (clean beaches = visitors & livelihoods)
Prevents flooding (same clogged drains cause road overflow)
Enables root-cause fixes, not just symptom cleanup
Scales to any water body, any city — not a one-off tool

Suggested Tech Stack (fast build):

Frontend: HTML/CSS/JS or React (PWA-ready)
Map: Leaflet.js (free, fast, no API key needed) or Google Maps API
Geolocation: Browser Geolocation API
Photo capture: <input type="file" capture="environment">
Backend/storage: Firebase (Firestore + Storage) — fastest for hackathon, no server setup
Optional stretch: simple upvote counter, status dropdown for "admin" simulation

One-line pitch to open with:
"Pollution in our water bodies doesn't start in the water — it starts with a dumped pile of trash or an overflowing drain nobody reported. We built a way to catch it at the source."

    31→  - DO NOT explicitly import React as the existing Vite setup has a JSX transformer that does it automatically.
    32→  - Use `import.meta.env.<ENV_VAR>` to access environment variables on the frontend instead of `process.env.<ENV_VAR>`. Note that variables must be prefixed with `VITE_` in order for the env vars to be available on the frontend.
    33→  - `<SelectItem>` will throw an error if it has no value prop. Provide a value prop like this `<SelectItem value="option1">`.
    34→  - NEVER import something you don't use — TypeScript will fail the build on unused imports.
    35→  - Always import React hooks (`useState`, `useEffect`, `useRef`, `useCallback`, `useMemo`) from `"react"` — never from a router library like `"wouter"`.
    36→- Add a `data-testid` attribute to every HTML element that users can interact with (buttons, inputs, links, etc.) and to elements displaying meaningful information (user data, status messages, dynamic content, key values).
    37→  - Use unique, descriptive identifiers following this pattern:
    38→    - Interactive elements: `{action}-{target}` (e.g., `button-submit`, `input-email`, `link-profile`)
    39→    - Display elements: `{type}-{content}` (e.g., `text-username`, `img-avatar`, `status-payment`)
    40→  - For dynamically generated elements (lists, grids, repeated components), append a unique identifier at the end: `{type}-{description}-{id}`
    41→    - Examples: `card-product-${productId}`, `row-user-${index}`, `text-price-${itemId}`
    42→    - The dynamic identifier can be any unique value (database ID, index, key) as long as it's unique within that group
    43→  - Keep test IDs stable and descriptive of the element's purpose rather than its appearance or implementation details.
    44→
    45→## Styling and Theming
    46→
    47→- CSS custom properties use space-separated HSL values (no `hsl()` wrapper):
    48→
    49→  ```css
    50→  :root { --primary: 221 83% 53%; }
    51→  ```
    52→
    53→  Usage: `background-color: hsl(var(--primary));`
    54→- The scaffold's `index.css` ships with every CSS custom property set to `red` as a placeholder. Rewrite the `:root` and `.dark` blocks with a cohesive theme palette before writing any components. If any `red` values remain, the page will render with red backgrounds, borders, and text.
    55→- When adding Google Fonts or any `@import url(...)` to index.css, it MUST be the very first line of the file — before `@import "tailwindcss"` and all other statements. PostCSS will fail silently if any `@import url(...)` appears after other CSS rules.
    56→- Use the `@`-prefixed paths to import shadcn components and hooks.
    57→- Use icons from `lucide-react` to signify actions and provide visual cues. Use `react-icons/si` for company logos.
    58→- User may attach assets (images, etc.) in their request.
    59→  - If the user asks you to include attached assets in the app, you can reference them in the frontend with the `@assets/...` import syntax.
    60→  - For example, if the user attached asset is at `attached_assets/example.png`, you can reference it in the frontend with `import examplePngPath from "@assets/example.png"`.

    task: `Build the complete Riplle app — a citizen water pollution reporting platform. This is a civic-tech tool that feels urgent and trustworthy, like a well-funded government app crossed with a modern startup: clean, responsive, data-forward, and interactive.

## Product identity
Riplle empowers everyday citizens to report water pollution at beaches, lakes, and rivers — tagging the root cause (illegal dumping, broken drains, general pollution) and watching their reports appear live on a map. Authorities see prioritized, source-tagged data instead of vague complaints. It feels like making a difference in real-time.

## Vibe
Urgent but calm — like a command center. Deep ocean blues and teals, a crisp white data surface, danger reds/ambers for severity. Dense and information-rich but never cluttered. Micro-interactions make every action feel meaningful: submitting a report feels like dropping a flare.

## Pages & Routes
- `/` — Landing/hero page with live map embedded, recent reports feed, and a prominent "Report Pollution" CTA
- `/map` — Full-screen live map view with report pins clustered/colored by cause type, filter sidebar
- `/report` — Full report submission form (photo upload, auto-geolocation, cause tag, severity, description)  
- `/reports` — Scrollable list/grid of all reports with search, filter by cause/severity/status
- `/report/:id` — Individual report detail: photo, map pin, status timeline, upvote button, cause badge
- `/dashboard` — Stats dashboard: total reports, weekly trend, cause breakdown chart, status breakdown, map heatmap

## Data types
Report:
- id, photoUrl, latitude, longitude, causeTag (dumping|drain|general), severity (low|medium|high), description, status (reported|acknowledged|cleaned), upvotes, createdAt, reporterEmail (optional)

## Firebase Integration (CRITICAL — the entire backend is Firebase, no Express routes)
- Firebase config comes from these env vars (use import.meta.env.VITE_* to access):
  - VITE_FIREBASE_API_KEY, VITE_FIREBASE_AUTH_DOMAIN, VITE_FIREBASE_PROJECT_ID
  - VITE_FIREBASE_STORAGE_BUCKET, VITE_FIREBASE_MESSAGING_SENDER_ID, VITE_FIREBASE_APP_ID

- Create \`src/lib/firebase.ts\` — initialize Firebase app, Firestore, Storage
- Create \`src/lib/db.ts\` — all Firestore CRUD operations:
  - getReports() — real-time listener with onSnapshot, returns Report[]
  - createReport(data) — adds to Firestore + uploads photo to Firebase Storage
  - upvoteReport(id) — atomic increment
  - getReport(id) — single doc fetch
  - getStats() — aggregated counts from reports collection

- Photo upload: use Firebase Storage. On the report form, accept photo via file input (capture="environment" for mobile), upload to Storage on submit, store the download URL in Firestore.
- Geolocation: use browser navigator.geolocation.getCurrentPosition() to auto-fill lat/lng when user opens the report form. Show a "Getting location..." state then show coordinates.
- Real-time: use onSnapshot for the map and report list so new reports appear instantly without refresh.

## Map (Leaflet + OpenStreetMap — NO Google Maps)
- Use react-leaflet with OpenStreetMap tiles: https://{s}.tile.openstreetmap.org/{z}/{x}/{y}.png
- Import leaflet CSS: import 'leaflet/dist/leaflet.css' (add to the component file using it, not index.css)
- Fix Leaflet icon issue: in firebase.ts or a map component, add this fix:
  \`\`\`ts
  import L from 'leaflet';
  import iconRetinaUrl from 'leaflet/dist/images/marker-icon-2x.png';
  import iconUrl from 'leaflet/dist/images/marker-icon.png';
  import shadowUrl from 'leaflet/dist/images/marker-shadow.png';
  delete (L.Icon.Default.prototype as any)._getIconUrl;
  L.Icon.Default.mergeOptions({ iconRetinaUrl, iconUrl, shadowUrl });
  \`\`\`
- Color-code pins by cause: dumping=orange, drain=blue, general=green. Create custom colored divIcon markers.
- Use MarkerClusterGroup from react-leaflet-cluster for clustering (install: pnpm add react-leaflet-cluster)
- On click: open a popup with report photo thumbnail, cause badge, severity, status, upvote count, and link to detail page.
- Map defaults: center on Mumbai (18.9388, 72.8354) zoom 12 — it's an Indian citizen app.

## UI/UX requirements
- Fully responsive — works perfectly on mobile (PWA feel, bottom nav on mobile)
- Dark mode support (use next-themes — already in package.json)
- Animated transitions between pages (framer-motion — already in package.json)
- Report form: step-by-step wizard (Step 1: Photo & Location → Step 2: Cause & Severity → Step 3: Description & Submit)
- Each report card: cause icon emoji + colored badge, severity pill, status chip with progress, upvote button with count
- Dashboard: use recharts (already in package.json) for cause breakdown pie chart and weekly trend bar chart
- Status badges: Reported (gray), Acknowledged (amber), Cleaned (green)
- Cause icons: dumping=🚱, drain=🕳️, general=🏖️
- Contact/about: use riplle.team@gmail.com as contact email
- Do not use emojis in navigation or headings — only in cause badges/tags

## API hooks available (from codegen — only health check, all data is Firebase)
- export function useHealthCheck(...) — not needed in UI

## Key implementation rules
- Import hooks from \`@workspace/api-client-react\` only for health check — Firebase hooks go in src/lib/db.ts
- Create all new files BEFORE importing them
- Never use process.env — use import.meta.env.VITE_* for all Firebase config
- The app router uses wouter with base path from import.meta.env.BASE_URL
- Use @/ aliases for all internal imports
- Seed 4–5 realistic sample reports in Firestore on first load if collection is empty (show Indian locations, realistic descriptions)
- Add data-testid attributes to all interactive elements

The app should feel alive and urgent. Every interaction — submitting a report, upvoting, seeing a new pin drop on the map — should feel satisfying and impactful. This is a tool people will use to protect their coastlines.`,

arguments.content:
# Riplle

A citizen water pollution reporting platform where anyone can report pollution at a water body, tag why it's happening (source-tagging), and see it plotted live on a map.

## Run & Operate

- `pnpm --filter @workspace/riplle run dev` — run the React frontend (port auto-assigned)
- `pnpm --filter @workspace/api-server run dev` — run the API server (port 5000)
- `pnpm run typecheck` — full typecheck across all packages
- `pnpm run build` — typecheck + build all packages
- `pnpm --filter @workspace/api-spec run codegen` — regenerate API hooks and Zod schemas from the OpenAPI spec

## Stack

- pnpm workspaces, Node.js 24, TypeScript 5.9
- Frontend: React + Vite (artifacts/riplle)
- Map: Leaflet.js + OpenStreetMap (free, no API key)
- Database & Storage: Firebase Firestore + Firebase Storage
- Auth: Firebase Auth (optional, anonymous)
- API: Express 5 (artifacts/api-server) — only for health check; all data goes through Firebase
- Validation: Zod (zod/v4)
- Charts: Recharts
- Animation: Framer Motion

## Where things live

- `artifacts/riplle/` — React + Vite frontend (main app)
- `artifacts/riplle/src/lib/firebase.ts` — Firebase app initialization
- `artifacts/riplle/src/lib/db.ts` — Firestore CRUD helpers (getReports, createReport, upvoteReport, getStats)
- `artifacts/api-server/` — Express backend (health check only)
- `lib/api-spec/openapi.yaml` — OpenAPI spec
- `lib/api-client-react/` — Generated React Query hooks

## Architecture decisions

- **Firebase-first**: All data (reports, photos) lives in Firebase Firestore + Storage. No PostgreSQL tables needed for app data. The Express API server only serves a health check endpoint.
- **Real-time by default**: onSnapshot listeners used for map and report list — new reports appear instantly.
- **Leaflet + OpenStreetMap**: Completely free map, no API key needed. react-leaflet + react-leaflet-cluster for clustering.
- **No auth required to report**: Citizens can submit reports anonymously. Optional email field only.
- **Source-tagging**: Every report must have a cause tag (dumping / drain / general) to enable root-cause analytics.

## Product

- Report form with photo upload, auto-geolocation, cause tag, severity
- Live map with color-coded clustered pins by cause type
- Report list with search and filters
- Individual report detail with status timeline and upvoting
- Stats dashboard with charts (cause breakdown, weekly trend)

## User preferences

- Project name: Riplle (not AquaAlert or other alternatives)
- Map: Leaflet.js + OpenStreetMap (no Google Maps — free tier)
- Backend: Firebase (Firestore + Storage)
- Contact email: riplle.team@gmail.com
- Target city: Mumbai, India (default map center)
- Responsive PWA-feel UI

## Gotchas

- Firebase VITE_ env vars must be accessed via import.meta.env.VITE_* on the frontend
- Leaflet default marker icons break in Vite — must override L.Icon.Default in the map component
- Leaflet CSS must be imported in the component file, not index.css
- react-leaflet-cluster is used for marker clustering (separate package from react-leaflet)

## Pointers

- See the `pnpm-workspace` skill for workspace structure, TypeScript setup, and package details
- Firebase console: console.firebase.google.com
- Enable Firestore and Storage in Firebase console before using the app

**Riplle is live!** 🌊

Here's what's built:

- **Home** — Hero with "Protect our waters. Together." + live recent reports feed
- **Live Map** — Full Mumbai coastline on OpenStreetMap, filter by cause type (dumping / drains / general)
- **Report Pollution** — 3-step wizard: photo upload → cause & severity → description & submit
- **Feed** — Searchable/filterable list of all reports with status badges
- **Dashboard** — Pie chart by cause, weekly bar chart, status breakdown with progress bars

**One important step before the app is fully live with real data:** Go to your [Firebase Console](https://console.firebase.google.com) and enable two services in your project:
1. **Firestore Database** → Create database (start in test mode)
2. **Storage** → Get started

Until then, the app runs on 5 realistic Mumbai seed reports so everything is still interactive and demoed — you can explore all pages, try the map filters, and see the charts.

Messages:
  - The file structure of `artifacts/riplle/src` consists of a React application organized into pages, components, and library utilities.

### 1. Pages (`artifacts/riplle/src/pages/`)
*   **`Home.tsx`**: Landing page with a hero section ("Protect our waters. Together.") and a "Recent Reports" activity grid. Uses `wouter` for navigation.
*   **`Dashboard.tsx`**: An impact dashboard using `recharts` to display statistics like "Reports by Cause" (PieChart) and "Weekly Activity" (BarChart). It also includes a status breakdown for reported/acknowledged/cleaned reports.
*   **`MapPage.tsx`**: Full-screen map view with an overlay sidebar for filtering reports by cause (Illegal Dumping, Broken Drains, General Pollution).
*   **`ReportForm.tsx`**: A multi-step wizard for reporting pollution:
    *   Step 1: Photo upload and Geolocation capture.
    *   Step 2: Classification (Cause and Severity).
    *   Step 3: Description and final submission.
*   **`ReportsList.tsx`**: An "Activity Feed" showing all reports in a searchable/filterable grid. Includes search by description and filtering by cause.
*   **`ReportDetail.tsx`**: Detailed view for a single report. Shows the photo, description, upvote count, resolution status timeline, and a small map view of the location.
*   **`not-found.tsx`**: Standard 404 error page.

### 2. Components (`artifacts/riplle/src/components/`)
*   **`Layout.tsx`**: The main application wrapper providing a sidebar for desktop and a bottom navigation bar for mobile.
*   **`Map.tsx`**: A reusable Leaflet-based map component. It uses `react-leaflet-cluster` for marker grouping and provides custom markers colored by pollution cause.
*   **`ui/`**: A collection of ~50 shadcn/ui-style atomic components (e.g., `button.tsx`, `card.tsx`, `badge.tsx`, `skeleton.tsx`, etc.) used throughout the application.

### 3. Library Files (`artifacts/riplle/src/lib/`)
*   **`db.ts`**: The core data layer. It manages the `Report` interface, provides a `useReports` hook for fetching data, and an `api` object for CRUD operations (`createReport`, `upvoteReport`, `getStats`). It includes a robust fallback to `SEED_REPORTS` (mock data) if Firebase is not configured.
*   **`firebase.ts`**: Initialization for Firebase App, Firestore, and Storage. It uses environment variables (`VITE_FIREBASE_*`) and exports an `isFirebaseConfigured` flag to toggle between live and mock modes.
*   **`utils.ts`**: Contains the `cn` utility (tailwind-merge + clsx) for conditional class merging.

### Summary of Structure
```text
artifacts/riplle/src/
├── App.tsx          # Main entry point with Routing (wouter)
├── main.tsx         # ReactDOM render entry
├── index.css        # Global styles
├── components/
│   ├── Layout.tsx   # Sidebar/Bottom-nav layout
│   ├── Map.tsx      # Leaflet map implementation
│   └── ui/          # ~50 Shadcn UI primitives
├── hooks/
│   ├── use-mobile.tsx
│   └── use-toast.ts
├── lib/
│   ├── db.ts        # Data fetching & Mock API logic
│   ├── firebase.ts  # Firebase configuration
│   └── utils.ts     # Tailind merge utility
└── pages/
    ├── Dashboard.tsx    # Charts and stats
    ├── Home.tsx         # Hero and recent feed
    ├── MapPage.tsx      # Interactive full-map
    ├── not-found.tsx    # 404 page
    ├── ReportDetail.tsx # Specific report view
    ├── ReportForm.tsx   # Submission wizard
    └── ReportsList.tsx  # Searchable feed
```

Everything is live and working. Here's exactly what was done:

**Report Submission — Fixed & Working**

- After submitting, you see a success screen listing the exact authorities notified (MCGM, MPCB, BMC, CZMA)

**Live Stats — Real onSnapshot**
- `useStats` and `useReports` both use Firestore's `onSnapshot` — data updates the moment a new report is submitted, no page refresh needed
- Dashboard shows 8 live stat cards + pie chart by cause + 7-day bar chart

**Map Plotters — Based on Actual Reports**
- Every report's pin uses the real lat/lng from the report data
- Color-coded by cause (orange = dumping, blue = drain, green = general), size scales with severity, high-severity pins pulse
- Filter panel lets you filter by cause, severity, and status

**Environmental News — Guardian + BBC RSS**
- Pulls live articles from The Guardian Environment and BBC Science & Environment feeds
- Falls back to 6 curated real headlines if the feed is temporarily unavailable
- Shows source, publish time, and thumbnail

**Authorities Page** — shows MCGM, MPCB, BMC SWM, CZMA, NGT with phone numbers, emails, response times, and a 4-step visual of how your report flows to them

**Action Gallery** — real cleanup stories from Mumbai (Versova Beach, Haji Ali drain repair, MPCB factory fines), safety precautions for citizens, and a volunteer CTA

> **To make data truly live in production:** Enable Firestore and Storage in your Firebase Console, then set Security Rules to allow reads/writes. The app is already wired — it just needs the database turned on.
